# Jido Architecture Review — Overview

Reviewed: jido v2.3.2 (`lib/`, ~30k LOC, 126 files), focusing on correctness, ease of use,
idiomatic Elixir, and OTP usage. One finding per file in this folder.

## What's good

The high-level architecture is genuinely strong and worth saying out loud:

- **Pure-agent / runtime-effects split.** `Jido.Agent` as an immutable struct with
  `cmd/2 :: (agent, action) -> {agent, directives}` and `Jido.AgentServer` as the effect
  interpreter is a clean Elm/Redux-style design. Decision logic is testable without OTP.
- **Instance-scoped supervision.** `use Jido, otp_app: ...` producing a per-instance
  supervisor (Registry, DynamicSupervisor, Task.Supervisor, RuntimeStore) is the right
  shape for multi-tenancy and async tests.
- **Logical (not supervisory) parent/child hierarchy** with explicit `ParentRef`, monitors,
  orphan/adoption semantics, and RuntimeStore-backed rehydration is well thought through.
- **Completion-as-state** rather than process death avoids classic "agent exits = done"
  races, and the `warn_if_normal_stop` guard shows real attention to user footguns.
- Compile-time validation in `use Jido.Agent` (route conflicts, duplicate state keys,
  plugin requirements) fails fast with good messages.

## Where it bites

The findings cluster into four themes:

1. **Concurrency model has real races** (findings 01, 02, 03): the off-process sync-call
   task can silently clobber state mutated by the drain loop; an unchecked
   `Task.Supervisor.start_child` can wedge the server permanently; `RunInstruction`
   blocks the GenServer despite the non-blocking drain-loop promise.
2. **The "default instance" doesn't exist** (finding 04): the first example in the
   AgentServer moduledoc crashes out of the box because `Jido.Registry` /
   `Jido.AgentSupervisor` / `Jido.TaskSupervisor` are never started.
3. **Non-OTP process management** (findings 05, 06, 07): bare `spawn`, unsupervised
   `GenServer.start`, `Process.exit(:kill)`, unawaited `Task.async` in `Application.start`.
4. **Ergonomic sharp edges** (findings 08–15): swallowed errors, nil-returning helpers
   that crash the drain loop, duplicated signal pipelines with behavioral divergence,
   doc/code drift.

## Severity index

| # | Finding | Severity |
|---|---------|----------|
| 01 | Sync-call task races the drain loop; lost agent-state updates | High |
| 02 | Unchecked `Task.Supervisor.start_child` can wedge the server forever | High |
| 03 | `RunInstruction` executes actions synchronously inside the GenServer | High |
| 04 | Default `Jido` instance referenced everywhere but never started | High |
| 05 | Unsupervised processes: bare `spawn`, `GenServer.start`, `:kill` | Medium |
| 06 | `Jido.Scheduler.cancel/1` blocks the agent up to ~6s per cron job | Medium |
| 07 | `Discovery.init_async` uses unawaited `Task.async` in `Application.start` | Low |
| 08 | `state/1` ships the entire internal State struct to callers | Medium |
| 09 | RuntimeStore funnels all reads of a public ETS table through one GenServer | Medium |
| 10 | `:permanent` restart silently resurrects agents with fresh state | Medium |
| 11 | Two divergent signal pipelines (call vs cast) with different behavior | Medium |
| 12 | `cmd/2` swallows invalid actions into an unobserved Error directive | Medium |
| 13 | `emit_to_parent/3` returns `nil`, which crashes the drain loop | Medium |
| 14 | Await helpers: mailbox pollution, hidden polling, raising streams | Low |
| 15 | Debug/docs drift and hot-path inefficiencies (regex per match, ring buffer) | Low |
title: F00: Jido Architecture Review — Overview
